title: Quantum Criticality in Monolayer Amorphous Carbon
authors: Rejaul SK, Hanning Zhang, Artem K Grebenko, Arsen Herasymchuk, Ranjith Shivajirao, Hongji Zhang, Abee Nelson, Zheng Jue Tong, Gagandeep Singh, Naoto Kimiuchi, Yuta Sato, Kazutomo Suenaga, Chee Tat Toh, Rudolf A Romer, Shaffique Adam, Oleg V. Yazyev, Barbaros Ozyilmaz, Bent Weber
abstract: Amorphous solids represent the extreme limit of broken translational symmetry, in which the absence of long-range order removes well-defined crystal momenta and invalidates the Bloch description of electronic states. Monolayer amorphous carbon (MAC) has emerged as a unique realization of a strictly two-dimensional (2D) amorphous lattice defined by a structurally contiguous but topologically disordered $sp^2$-bonded random network devoid of any defined long-range crystal symmetry. From atomic-resolution measurements of multifractal wavefunctions, we show that disorder in MAC effectively localizes the low-energy part of the electronic spectrum but retains an extended critical-like state near the band centre ($E\sim 0$). We conjecture that this state is protected from topological disorder by remnant chiral symmetry surviving within the continuous random network, described by a Wess-Zumino-Witten (WZW) topological term. Near criticality, we verify the multifractal scaling relation $η= -Δ_2$, providing quantitative agreement between independently measured spatial correlation decay and multifractal scaling exponents. Our results are confirmed by atomistic tight-binding calculations that closely mirror the multifractal scaling near $E\sim 0$. Our results establish MAC as the first strictly 2D amorphous electronic system to exhibit Anderson criticality driven purely by topological disorder
summary: This paper demonstrates that monolayer amorphous carbon hosts scale-invariant, critical-like electronic states despite being a strictly 2D disordered system. This phenomenon is attributed to an emergent chiral symmetry protected by the topological disorder of the carbon network. The findings challenge the conventional view that all states in 2D are localized by weak disorder.
main_problem: The study investigates the nature of electronic localization in a strictly 2D amorphous system and whether topological disorder can prevent Anderson localization at the band center.
main_result: The authors discover that monolayer amorphous carbon (MAC) exhibits critical-like extended states near the band center, protected from localization by an emergent chiral symmetry described by a Wess-Zumino-Witten topological term.
method: The researchers combined experimental Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM/STS) and High-Resolution TEM with atomistic tight-binding simulations and multifractal analysis.
model_or_system: The system is Monolayer Amorphous Carbon (MAC), a 2D continuous random network of sp2-bonded carbon characterized by topological disorder (5-8 membered rings) and a lack of long-range translational symmetry.
key_observables: Local Density of States (LDOS) distributions, multifractal singularity spectrum f(alpha), anomalous scaling dimension Delta q, and localization length xi(E).
important_parameters: Energy near the band center (E ~ 0), critical exponent nu approx 0.90, and the scaling relation eta = -Delta2.
main_assumptions: The study assumes the disorder is primarily topological (bond-length/angle variations) rather than random on-site potentials, and proposes a WZW topological term in the effective field theory.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares LDOS maps and structural disorder in graphene vs MAC, showing non-periodic modulations and non-hexagonal rings. Figure 2 demonstrates the transition from Gaussian to log-normal LDOS distributions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of 2D localization, presents structural and electronic characterization of MAC via STM/TEM, details the multifractal and scaling analysis of wavefunctions, provides theoretical validation via tight-binding simulations, and concludes with a proposed topological mechanism for the observed criticality.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Microscopic signatures of critical wavefunctions in monolayer amorphous carbon: a,b, Constant current dI/dV maps on graphene on SiC, comparing a defect-free area (a) and a region with a point defect (b), give rise to quasiparticle interference (QPI). c, A constant current dI/dV map on MAC, showing significant spatial modulations in the LDOS amplitude, indicative of a critical-like fluctuation. The insets show the corresponding FFTs (Scale bar = 3 nm−1. d, High-resolution Trans- mission electron microscope (HR-TEM) image of MAC, highlighting the topological disorder , defined by random distribution of bond length and bond angle of non-hexagonal rings. e,f, STM topographies of MAC...

Fluctuation and multifractal scaling of the wavefunction probability density. a-d, Measured dI/dV maps of Gr/SiC (a), Gr/SiO2 (b) and, MAC (c) compared to the theoretically calculated LDOS (d). While graphene shows a homogeneous LDOS distribution, MAC exhibits strong spatial fluctuations. e-h, This is further illustrated by histograms of normalized LDOS, showing Gaussian distributions (e,f) in graphene with a mean at 1.0, whereas MAC exhibits a lognormal distribution, indicative of critical-like electronic fluctuations and incipient localization.i,j, Measured (i) and calculated (j) singularity spectra obtained from the measured and numerically calculated LDOS maps shown in a-d,.
